core_claim: Fragnesia (CVE-2026-46300) is the third Linux kernel LPE in two weeks using page-cache corruption. It targets XFRM ESP-in-TCP subsystem. Same mitigation as Dirty Frag (blocklist esp4, esp6, rxrpc). No host-level privileges required unlike Dirty Frag. PoC released by V12 Security.

slides.visual_prompt: Low raking light grazes a dark kernel map in the dark, three red nodes pulsing in the XFRM/ESP region: Dirty Frag (ESP-in-UDP), Dirty Frag (RxRPC), Fragnesia (ESP-in-TCP), dark editorial photography, the map set high over a calm empty lower third.

slides.on_slide_copy: Modifies read-only file contents in kernel page cache. Achieves root via deterministic byte writes. Unlike Dirty Frag, no host-level privileges required. AppArmor namespace restrictions only partial mitigation.
slides.subline: Immediate root on all major distros by corrupting /usr/bin/su page cache. Third such bug in two weeks after Copy Fail and Dirty Frag.
slides.visual_direction: cinematic, dark, single accent glow; text-free background; protected lower-third.
slides.visual_prompt: A focused side light illuminates a scalpel in the dark making a precise incision into a locked file, the file contents rewriting in red without the lock triggering, dark premium editorial, the scalpel centered in the upper area with generous negative space below.

slides.on_slide_copy: Abuses logic bug in XFRM ESP-in-TCP subsystem. Achieves arbitrary byte writes into kernel page cache of read-only files. No race condition. Same outcome as Copy Fail and Dirty Frag: root via /usr/bin/su corruption.
slides.subline: CloudLinux: Customers with Dirty Frag mitigation need no further action until patched kernels. Red Hat assessing if mitigation extends.
slides.visual_direction: cinematic, dark, single accent glow; text-free background; protected lower-third.
slides.visual_prompt: A hard top-down beam falls on a TCP packet in the dark being decrypted in-place over shared page-cache pages, the plaintext bleeding into a read-only file reference, dark editorial key art, the packet framed in the upper third over clean dark space.

slides.on_slide_copy: Copy Fail (algif_aead) -> Dirty Frag (xfrm-ESP + RxRPC) -> Fragnesia (xfrm ESP-in-TCP). Three distinct subsystems. Same page-cache corruption primitive. Same root via /usr/bin/su. Same mitigation: blocklist esp4, esp6, rxrpc.
slides.subline: Threat actor 'berz0k' advertising Linux LPE zero-day for $170,000 on cybercrime forums. Claims TOCTOU-based, stable, no crashes, .so payload in /tmp.
slides.visual_direction: cinematic, dark, single accent glow; text-free background; protected lower-third.
slides.visual_prompt: Cool light grazes an assembly line in the dark producing identical root exploits from three different subsystem inputs: ALGIF_AEAD, XFRM_UDP, XFRM_TCP, all outputting the same ROOT product, tense editorial composition, the line held high with a clean lower third.

slides.on_slide_copy: Blocklist esp4, esp6, rxrpc modules. Restrict unprivileged user namespaces. Harden container workloads. Monitor for abnormal privilege escalation. Apply kernel patches when available.
slides.subline: Microsoft urges same Dirty Frag mitigations. Wiz notes AppArmor namespace restrictions only partial bypass needed. No in-the-wild exploitation observed yet.
slides.visual_direction: cinematic, dark, single accent glow; text-free background; protected lower-third.

caption:
Fragnesia (CVE-2026-46300) is the third Linux kernel LPE in two weeks using page-cache corruption. Targets XFRM ESP-in-TCP subsystem. Discovered by William Bowling of Zellic and V12 Security.

Separate bug from Dirty Frag but same surface and same mitigation. Deterministic page-cache corruption. No race condition. Immediate root on all major distros by corrupting /usr/bin/su page cache.

Unlike Dirty Frag, no host-level privileges required. AppArmor namespace restrictions only partial mitigation — additional bypasses needed.

The pattern: Copy Fail (algif_aead) -> Dirty Frag (xfrm-ESP + RxRPC) -> Fragnesia (xfrm ESP-in-TCP). Three distinct subsystems. Same page-cache corruption primitive. Same root via /usr/bin/su. Same mitigation: blocklist esp4, esp6, rxrpc.

CloudLinux: Customers with Dirty Frag mitigation need no further action until patched kernels. Red Hat assessing if mitigation extends. Microsoft urges same mitigations. No in-the-wild exploitation observed yet.

Threat actor 'berz0k' advertising Linux LPE zero-day for $170,000 on cybercrime forums. Claims TOCTOU-based, stable, no crashes, .so payload in /tmp.

The page-cache corruption bug class spans three subsystems now. One mitigation covers all three. But the kernel keeps finding new paths to the same primitive.

How many page-cache corruption primitives are in your kernel right now?

Follow for AI security breakdowns without the fake panic.

[Fragnesia, CVE-2026-46300, Linux kernel, local privilege escalation, page cache, XFRM ESP-in-TCP, Dirty Frag, Copy Fail, kernel hardening, V12 Security]
comment_prompt: How many page-cache corruption primitives are in your kernel right now?
alt_text: Title card: Third page-cache LPE in two weeks, same root, same mitigation. Fragnesia is CVE-2026-46300 in XFRM ESP-in-TCP, no host privileges needed, Dirty Frag mitigation works. Three shards fuse into one red blade.

Fragnesia targets the XFRM ESP-in-TCP subsystem, found by William Bowling of Zellic and V12 Security. CVE-2026-46300, CVSS 7.8, deterministic page-cache corruption, no race condition. A dark kernel map with three red nodes.

It modifies read-only file contents in the kernel page cache to gain root via deterministic byte writes. Unlike Dirty Frag, no host privileges are required and AppArmor is only partial mitigation. A scalpel cuts a locked file.

It abuses a logic bug in XFRM ESP-in-TCP for arbitrary byte writes into the page cache of read-only files, no race condition, root via /usr/bin/su corruption. A TCP packet decrypts in place over shared page-cache pages.

The pattern: Copy Fail, then Dirty Frag, then Fragnesia. Three subsystems, same page-cache primitive, same root, same mitigation: blocklist esp4, esp6, rxrpc. An assembly line outputs identical root exploits from three inputs.

Mitigation: blocklist esp4, esp6, rxrpc, restrict unprivileged user namespaces, harden containers, monitor for escalation, and patch the kernel when available. Three converging bugs are stopped by one module blocklist.

The page-cache corruption bug class now spans three subsystems. One mitigation covers all three, but the kernel keeps finding new paths to the same primitive. Three roots merge into a trunk labeled page-cache corruption.
Closing question: How many page-cache corruption primitives are in your kernel right now? A kernel subsystem map shows three red paths converging on the page cache.

narration.text: Third page-cache LPE in two weeks. Fragnesia. XFRM ESP-in-TCP. Same root. Same mitigation.
Discovered by Zellic and V12. Separate from Dirty Frag but same surface. Deterministic. No race condition.
Modifies read-only file page cache. Root via su corruption. Unlike Dirty Frag, no host privileges needed.
Pattern: Copy Fail algif_aead. Dirty Frag xfrm-ESP and RxRPC. Fragnesia xfrm ESP-in-TCP. Three subsystems. Same primitive.
Same mitigation: blocklist esp4, esp6, rxrpc. CloudLinux says Dirty Frag mitigation covers Fragnesia. Red Hat assessing.
Threat actor berz0k selling Linux LPE for $170k. TOCTOU-based. Stable. .so payload in /tmp. Microsoft urges mitigations.
Page-cache corruption bug class spans three subsystems. One mitigation covers all. Kernel keeps finding new paths.
How many page-cache corruption primitives in your kernel right now? Tell me in the comments.

beats.caption: Third page-cache LPE in two weeks. Same root.
Zellic/V12 discovery. Separate bug. Same surface.
No host privileges needed. AppArmor only partial.
Three subsystems. One primitive. algif_aead, xfrm UDP, xfrm TCP.
Same mitigation covers all three. berz0k selling $170k zero-day.
Blocklist esp4/esp6/rxrpc. Harden containers. Patch kernel.
Bug class spans three subsystems. Kernel finds new paths.
